stem: You are the community mental health team registrar reviewing a 26-year-old woman with borderline personality disorder. She has had 4 self-harm episodes by superficial cutting in the last 6 weeks and 1 emergency presentation with paracetamol ingestion of 8 g 3 weeks ago, managed conservatively. She is currently on no psychotropic medication and is willing to engage in psychological therapy. She has chronic feelings of emptiness, fear of abandonment and intense unstable relationships. There are no psychotic features.

data_table.Examination: Multiple superficial healing forearm lacerations; mood reactive; no psychosis; no acute suicidality at interview
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFTs (post-paracetamol) normal; urine drug screen negative

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line management strategy?

options.text: Refer for dialectical behaviour therapy with weekly individual and weekly skills group for at least 12 months
options.rationale: DBT is the most evidence-based first-line treatment for BPD with recurrent self-harm, with reductions in self-harm, suicide attempts and hospitalisation in randomised trials. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Commence quetiapine 100 mg nocte; arrange GP review in 4 weeks; defer psychological therapy until mood is more stable
options.rationale: Medication is not first-line for BPD and quetiapine is not indicated for emotion dysregulation alone; psychological therapy is the primary treatment. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Refer for cognitive behavioural therapy of 12 sessions focused on cognitive restructuring for self-harm thoughts
options.rationale: Standard CBT is less effective for BPD than structured therapies (DBT, MBT, schema-focused therapy); 12 sessions is also too short. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Admit for inpatient stabilisation under the Mental Health Act; arrange psychiatric review for medication initiation
options.rationale: Inpatient admission is generally avoided in BPD outside of brief crisis admission; involuntary admission and routine pharmacotherapy worsen long-term outcomes. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily; refer to GP-led mental health treatment plan for 10 sessions of supportive counselling
options.rationale: SSRI is not first-line for BPD without comorbid major depression; supportive counselling is less effective than structured BPD-specific therapy. (Source: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD)
source_refs: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

explanation.summary: The NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD recommends structured psychological therapy such as DBT, MBT or schema-focused therapy as first-line treatment, particularly with recurrent self-harm. DBT has the strongest evidence and reduces self-harm, suicide attempts and inpatient days. Medication is reserved for clearly identified comorbid disorders and is not the primary treatment for BPD itself. Prolonged inpatient admission is generally avoided due to risk of regression and dependency.
key_points: DBT, MBT and schema-focused therapy are first-line for BPD
Pharmacotherapy is for comorbid conditions, not BPD per se
Avoid prolonged inpatient admission
Standard CBT and supportive counselling are less effective
explanation.pearls: Standard DBT comprises weekly individual therapy, weekly skills group, phone coaching and therapist consultation team for at least 12 months.

sources.label: NHMRC Clinical Practice Guideline for BPD

stem: You are the emergency registrar reviewing a 34-year-old woman who took her dosette tray of amitriptyline 50 mg tablets approximately 90 minutes ago after a relationship breakup. She estimates 28 tablets ingested. On arrival she is drowsy but rousable, with dry mucous membranes and an ECG showing sinus tachycardia. She has just had one self-terminating generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 45 seconds. The ECG is in front of you.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58, HR 132, RR 18, SpO2 96% on 6 L Hudson mask, T 37.2, GCS 12 (E3 V4 M5)
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, dilated pupils, hyperreflexia, no focal deficit, no airway compromise after seizure
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia HR 132, QRS 140 ms, terminal R wave aVR 5 mm; pH 7.28, lactate 3.4, K 3.6, paracetamol negative
data_table.Medications: Amitriptyline 50 mg nocte usually; no other regular medications

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer flumazenil 200 micrograms IV; commence intravenous normal saline 1 L over 30 minutes and recheck ECG
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose or TCA toxicity due to seizure precipitation; the drowsiness here is anticholinergic plus TCA, not benzodiazepine. (Source: AMH: Flumazenil)

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol IV bolus and prepare an infusion; intubate if GCS drops or seizures recur
options.rationale: QRS above 100 ms in TCA toxicity is an indication for sodium bicarbonate; bolus 1 to 2 mmol/kg with reassessment is the standard antidote and reduces cardiotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer activated charcoal 50 g orally; commence intravenous diazepam 5 mg for sedation and seizure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Activated charcoal in a drowsy patient without secured airway risks aspiration; benzodiazepines treat established seizures but bicarbonate is the priority for cardiotoxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 10 minutes; commence amiodarone 300 mg infusion for arrhythmia prophylaxis
options.rationale: Magnesium has no evidence in TCA toxicity and amiodarone can worsen sodium channel blockade; class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Administer intravenous lipid emulsion 1.5 mL/kg over 1 minute; commence noradrenaline infusion for hypotension control
options.rationale: Intralipid is rescue therapy in refractory TCA cardiac arrest; not first line; sodium bicarbonate precedes it. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: TCA toxicity causes sodium and potassium channel blockade leading to QRS widening, prolonged QT and ventricular arrhythmia, plus anticholinergic features and seizures. Indications for sodium bicarbonate include QRS above 100 ms, terminal R in aVR greater than 3 mm, ventricular dysrhythmia or hypotension. Standard regimen is 1 to 2 mmol/kg bolus followed by infusion to target pH 7.5. Avoid class IA, IC and III antiarrhythmics; treat seizures with benzodiazepines; intralipid is reserved for refractory arrest.
key_points: QRS above 100 ms is the trigger for sodium bicarbonate in TCA toxicity
Target arterial pH 7.5 with bicarbonate infusion
Avoid amiodarone, lignocaine and procainamide
Intralipid is rescue therapy in refractory arrest
explanation.pearls: Bicarbonate 1 to 2 mmol/kg IV bolus, repeat every 5 minutes if QRS remains widened, then infusion at 100 to 150 mmol in 1 L D5W at 250 mL/h.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar assessing a 31-year-old man with a 6-year illness. He has had 3 manic episodes with mood-congruent grandiose delusions, each followed by 6 to 12 months of residual auditory hallucinations and persecutory delusions without significant mood symptoms. Between episodes he has not returned to premorbid function and has continuing negative symptoms. His family history is significant for both bipolar and schizophrenia. He is currently on no medication after self-discontinuation 6 months ago.

data_table.Examination: Flat affect, third person auditory hallucinations, persecutory delusions, no current mania, no current depression
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFTs, TSH, urine drug screen normal; CT brain no acute change

lead_in: Which DSM-5-TR diagnosis is most consistent with this clinical course?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features and a current episode in partial remission with residual symptoms
options.rationale: Bipolar I requires psychotic features to be confined to mood episodes; psychosis persisting for 6 to 12 months without prominent mood disturbance does not fit. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia, paranoid type, with secondary mood episodes related to illness burden rather than primary affective disorder
options.rationale: Paranoid type is no longer a DSM-5-TR specifier; full manic episodes with mood-congruent psychosis weigh against primary schizophrenia. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, with mood episodes and at least 2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood symptoms
options.rationale: Schizoaffective disorder requires at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms without major mood episode and mood episodes for a substantial portion of total illness; this case fits the bipolar subtype. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder evolving towards schizophrenia, with affective symptoms attributable to chronic illness burden
options.rationale: Schizophreniform is by definition shorter than 6 months and does not feature full manic episodes; ill-fitting given the 6-year course. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with persistent inter-episode psychotic symptoms and high familial loading for both bipolar and schizophrenia
options.rationale: Persistent inter-episode psychosis crosses the boundary out of bipolar disorder into schizoaffective territory; the diagnosis should reflect the longitudinal course. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: DSM-5-TR distinguishes schizoaffective disorder from bipolar I with psychotic features by the longitudinal pattern of psychosis. Schizoaffective requires at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms without prominent mood disturbance during the lifetime course, plus mood episodes for a substantial portion of total illness duration. The bipolar subtype includes manic episodes; the depressive subtype is restricted to major depressive episodes. Treatment usually combines an antipsychotic with a mood stabiliser.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires at least 2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms
Mood episodes must occur during a substantial portion of total illness
Bipolar subtype includes mania; depressive subtype is MDE-only
Treatment combines antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser
explanation.pearls: Substantial portion is not numerically defined in DSM-5-TR but typically interpreted as at least one third to half of total active illness time.

stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 28-year-old teacher with 4 weeks of right leg weakness preventing her from walking unassisted. There is no antecedent trauma. She is currently on workers' compensation for unrelated stress leave. Examination shows Hoover sign positive on the right leg, give-way weakness throughout the right leg with normal tone and reflexes, and a non-anatomical sensory loss. MRI brain and spine are normal. Nerve conduction studies are normal.

data_table.Examination: Right leg 2/5 power on direct testing with give-way pattern; Hoover sign positive; sensory loss to fingertip threshold non-anatomical; reflexes 2+; toes downgoing
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and spine normal; NCS and EMG normal; B12, TSH, glucose normal

lead_in: Which statement most accurately describes the diagnostic framework?

options.text: Defer diagnosis; arrange repeat MRI with contrast and lumbar puncture in 4 weeks to exclude inflammatory disease conclusively
options.rationale: Repeated negative investigations reinforce the sick role and delay treatment; FND is a positive diagnosis not requiring exclusion of all alternatives. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Somatic Symptom Disorders)

options.text: Diagnose malingering based on the workers' compensation context and refer to occupational physician for capacity assessment
options.rationale: Malingering requires intentional production of symptoms for external gain; the patient cannot consciously generate a positive Hoover sign and presence of secondary gain does not establish intentionality. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Diagnose factitious disorder imposed on self based on absence of physical findings and presence of psychosocial stressor
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires conscious deception without external gain; positive clinical signs and absence of evidence of deception point to FND. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Diagnose functional neurological disorder based on positive Hoover sign and rule-in clinical features; explain the diagnosis collaboratively
options.rationale: FND in DSM-5-TR is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical signs such as Hoover sign and tremor entrainment; it does not require exclusion of all organic disease nor evidence of psychological stressor. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Somatic Symptom Disorders)

options.text: Diagnose generalised anxiety disorder based on the work-related stress; commence sertraline 50 mg daily and refer for CBT
options.rationale: GAD does not cause focal weakness with positive Hoover sign; SSRI is not first-line treatment for FND without comorbid anxiety. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Somatic Symptom Disorders)

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is a positive diagnosis in DSM-5-TR based on clinical signs such as Hoover sign, hip abductor sign, tremor entrainment and dissociative seizure features. Psychological stressor is no longer required for diagnosis. Communication of the diagnosis using clear language and pathophysiological explanation improves outcomes. First-line treatment involves physiotherapy for motor FND and CBT for cognitive aspects; antidepressants are reserved for comorbid mood or anxiety disorders.
key_points: FND is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical signs
Psychological stressor is no longer a DSM-5-TR diagnostic requirement
Repeated investigations reinforce illness; collaborative explanation is first step
Physiotherapy and CBT are mainstay treatments
explanation.pearls: Hoover sign: passive hip extension power on the weak side is restored when the patient is asked to flex the contralateral hip against resistance, indicating selective central engagement.

subtopic_detail: Refeeding syndrome prevention in severe anorexia
stem: You are the medical registrar admitting a 19-year-old woman with anorexia nervosa restricting subtype. BMI is 13.4, she has been eating less than 500 kcal per day for 8 weeks and her phosphate on admission is 0.65 mmol/L. She has no oedema. Her ECG shows sinus bradycardia at 38 with QTc 470 ms. She is awake and oriented. The eating disorder service has requested admission for refeeding under their protocol.

data_table.Examination: Cachectic, lanugo, cool peripheries, no peripheral oedema, mild orthostatic drop
data_table.Investigations: Na 140, K 3.1, phosphate 0.65 (low), Mg 0.62 (low), Cr 64, glucose 3.4, Hb 102, WCC 3.2, LFTs ALT 78, ALP 92, INR 1.1, ECG QTc 470, HR 38

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial refeeding strategy?

options.text: Commence enteral feed at 30 kcal/kg per day with daily electrolyte monitoring; replace phosphate and magnesium intravenously
options.rationale: 30 kcal/kg/day at this BMI exceeds safe starting rate per ANZAED guidance and risks severe refeeding hypophosphataemia; starting rate should be 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day in highest-risk patients. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Defer feeding for 24 hours; correct electrolytes first then start enteral feed at 20 kcal/kg per day with weekly electrolytes
options.rationale: Delaying nutrition does not prevent refeeding syndrome; structured low-rate feeding with concurrent electrolyte replacement is the standard approach with daily electrolyte monitoring. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Commence parenteral nutrition at 25 kcal/kg per day; replace electrolytes intravenously and start propranolol for bradycardia
options.rationale: Enteral is preferred over parenteral when the gut works; beta blockade in extreme bradycardia of anorexia is dangerous and contraindicated. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral diet ad libitum with appetite-led portions; check phosphate and magnesium twice weekly during refeeding admission
options.rationale: Ad libitum feeding in severe anorexia risks rapid refeeding syndrome and is not standard; structured low-rate refeeding with daily electrolytes is required. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guidelines)

options.text: Commence oral feed at 5 to 10 kcal/kg per day with cardiac monitoring; replace phosphate, magnesium and potassium and give thiamine 200 mg IV daily for 3 days
options.rationale: BMI under 14 with hypophosphataemia and bradycardia is highest refeeding risk; start at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day, replace electrolytes pre-emptively and give thiamine 100 to 300 mg IV daily for the first 3 to 5 days. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome risk is highest in BMI under 14, weight loss greater than 15 percent in 3 months, minimal intake for more than 10 days and pre-existing electrolyte derangement. ANZAED guidance recommends starting at 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day in highest-risk patients with daily electrolyte monitoring and pre-emptive replacement of phosphate, magnesium and potassium. Thiamine 100 to 300 mg IV daily for 3 to 5 days precedes carbohydrate to prevent Wernicke's encephalopathy. Calorie titration upwards is guided by electrolyte stability and clinical tolerance.
key_points: BMI under 14 is highest refeeding risk - start 5 to 10 kcal/kg/day
Pre-emptive phosphate, magnesium and potassium replacement
Thiamine before glucose load
Daily electrolyte monitoring in first week of refeeding
explanation.pearls: Phosphate falls 1 to 3 days into refeeding; check daily and replace IV if below 0.6 mmol/L or symptomatic at any level.

stem: You are the medical registrar called to ED to review a 72-year-old man with 2 days of dysuria, rigors and now reduced level of consciousness. He has a history of BPH and recent urinary catheterisation 1 week ago for retention. His skin is mottled and his hands are cool. He has been triaged to resus.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 86/48, HR 124, RR 28, SpO2 92% on room air, T 38.9, GCS 13
data_table.Examination: Mottled extremities, cool peripheries, mild suprapubic tenderness, no flank tenderness; chest clear, no rash
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 4.2, WCC 18.6, neutrophils 16.1, CRP 240, Cr 168 (baseline 88), urine dipstick nitrites +, leukocytes ++; ABG pH 7.28, HCO3 18; blood cultures and urine cultures collected
data_table.Medications: Tamsulosin 400 micrograms daily; finasteride 5 mg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic and resuscitation in the first hour?

options.text: IV gentamicin 5 mg/kg with amoxicillin 2 g and 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus over 1 hour; arrange ICU review for vasopressor
options.rationale: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures, broad spectrum antibiotic within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4, lactate and ICU escalation; gentamicin plus amoxicillin covers community-onset urosepsis in Australian practice. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ceftriaxone 1 g daily with metronidazole 500 mg and 1 L crystalloid over 4 hours; arrange ward bed and senior review
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone 1 g underdoses serious sepsis; metronidazole not required for urosepsis; 1 L over 4 hours is insufficient for septic shock; ward disposition undertreats severity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g and 250 mL hypertonic saline bolus over 30 minutes; commence noradrenaline at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not first line in septic shock resuscitation; balanced crystalloid 30 mL/kg precedes vasopressor; pip-taz is acceptable but balanced fluid is preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV cefalexin 1 g and 500 mL crystalloid bolus over 30 minutes; defer further escalation until inpatient team reviews
options.rationale: Oral or narrow gram-positive cefalexin underdoses urosepsis and oral route is inappropriate in shock; deferring escalation breaches hour-1 standard. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV ciprofloxacin 400 mg and 2 L crystalloid bolus over 2 hours; reassess and consider noradrenaline if BP remains below 90 systolic
options.rationale: Ciprofloxacin monotherapy is not first-line for severe urosepsis in Australia given fluoroquinolone resistance; bolus over 2 hours is too slow for septic shock. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 bundle comprises lactate measurement, blood cultures before antibiotics where possible, broad-spectrum antibiotic, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate above 4, and vasopressor for persistent hypotension. Australian empirical therapy for serious urosepsis is gentamicin plus amoxicillin in the absence of beta-lactam allergy or ESBL risk. Lactate above 4 mmol/L is a key trigger for aggressive resuscitation. ICU escalation is required for septic shock not responding to initial fluid.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotic, 30 mL/kg fluid, vasopressor
Empirical urosepsis: gentamicin plus amoxicillin in Australia
Balanced crystalloid 30 mL/kg over the first hour
Lactate above 4 indicates need for aggressive resuscitation
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin 5 mg/kg single dose by lean body weight is the Australian standard for severe gram-negative sepsis; redose at 24 hours guided by levels.

stem: You are the ICU registrar managing a 64-year-old woman with septic shock from a perforated diverticular abscess. After 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, her MAP is 58 mmHg and she remains tachycardic at 118. She has had source control surgery 2 hours ago and is on piperacillin-tazobactam plus gentamicin. She has a central line in situ. Her ScvO2 is 72 percent and her lactate has fallen from 4.6 to 3.2.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/40 (MAP 58), HR 118, RR 22 ventilated, SpO2 96% on 40% FiO2, T 37.4
data_table.Examination: Warm extremities, cap refill 3 s, urine output 0.4 mL/kg/h over last hour, no new bleeding
data_table.Investigations: Lactate 3.2 (down from 4.6), Hb 96, K 4.2, ScvO2 72%, echo bedside shows hyperdynamic LV, IVC variable
data_table.Medications: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours; gentamicin loaded

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence dopamine at 5 micrograms/kg/min titrated to MAP 65 mmHg with arterial line monitoring in place
options.rationale: Dopamine is no longer recommended due to greater arrhythmia risk and worse outcomes compared with noradrenaline in septic shock. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min titrated to MAP 65 mmHg with arterial line monitoring
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor for septic shock with MAP below 65 after adequate fluid resuscitation per Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence vasopressin 0.03 units/min as monotherapy; reassess MAP after 30 minutes of stable infusion before escalation
options.rationale: Vasopressin is added when noradrenaline cannot maintain MAP, not as first-line monotherapy; pure vasopressin can cause coronary and digit ischaemia at higher doses. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer further 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus and reassess; defer vasopressor pending response to volume resuscitation
options.rationale: After 30 mL/kg with hyperdynamic LV and variable IVC, further large volume risks pulmonary oedema; vasopressor support is appropriate. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence adrenaline at 0.05 micrograms/kg/min; reassess MAP and lactate clearance after 2 hours of infusion at steady state
options.rationale: Adrenaline is reserved for shock not responding to noradrenaline plus vasopressin and can worsen lactate due to beta-mediated metabolic effects. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 recommends noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor for septic shock not responding to 30 mL/kg crystalloid. The MAP target is at least 65 mmHg. Vasopressin 0.03 units/min may be added to noradrenaline if MAP cannot be maintained or to reduce noradrenaline dose. Adrenaline is third line. Dopamine is no longer recommended due to arrhythmia risk and worse outcomes.
key_points: Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor in septic shock
Target MAP at least 65 mmHg
Vasopressin is added second line at 0.03 units/min
Avoid dopamine in septic shock
explanation.pearls: Hydrocortisone 200 mg per day IV is recommended in septic shock requiring ongoing vasopressor support; consider when noradrenaline is above 0.25 micrograms/kg/min.

stem: You are the medical registrar called to ED to review a 42-year-old woman with Addison's disease who presents with 36 hours of vomiting and diarrhoea from suspected viral gastroenteritis. She has not taken her usual hydrocortisone for 24 hours due to vomiting. She is on hydrocortisone 20 mg morning and 10 mg afternoon and fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucosa, sunken eyes, cool peripheries, hyperpigmentation of palmar creases, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Na 124, K 5.8, glucose 2.8, Cr 142 (baseline 64), Hb 142, CRP 18, lactate 2.4, cortisol pending
data_table.Medications: Hydrocortisone 20 mg morning, 10 mg afternoon; fludrocortisone 100 micrograms daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral hydrocortisone 40 mg with ondansetron 4 mg sublingual and 500 mL crystalloid; observe over 4 hours in ED short stay
options.rationale: Oral hydrocortisone is unreliable in vomiting and shock; IV route is mandatory; ED short stay underestimates severity. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Dexamethasone 4 mg IV bolus, 1 L Hartmann's bolus, and oral glucose drinks; admit for slow-stress dosing taper
options.rationale: Dexamethasone has minimal mineralocorticoid activity and is less appropriate; oral glucose in shock is unreliable; hydrocortisone is preferred. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus, 1 L 0.9% saline bolus, and 100 mL 10% glucose bolus; admit for ongoing replacement
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis requires immediate hydrocortisone 100 mg IV, isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, and glucose for hypoglycaemia; this is the standard ESA and Endocrine Society approach. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Fludrocortisone 200 micrograms oral, 1 L saline bolus, and oral glucose; await cortisol level before parenteral steroid
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone has no acute glucocorticoid effect; awaiting cortisol delays life-saving treatment; hydrocortisone is given empirically. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

options.text: Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV bolus, 2 L 5% dextrose bolus, and noradrenaline infusion for hypotension control
options.rationale: Methylprednisolone is acceptable but hydrocortisone is preferred for mineralocorticoid effect; pure dextrose without sodium worsens hyponatraemia; vasopressor before steroid is wrong order. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency)
source_refs: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis is a life-threatening emergency presenting with hypotension, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia and hyperkalaemia in a patient with known or unrecognised adrenal insufficiency. Immediate management is hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus followed by 200 mg over 24 hours, isotonic crystalloid resuscitation and glucose for hypoglycaemia. Cortisol level can be sent but treatment should not wait. Sick-day rules require patients to double oral hydrocortisone for febrile illness and use IM emergency hydrocortisone for vomiting.
key_points: Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus is first step in adrenal crisis
Isotonic saline resuscitation, not dextrose alone
Treat glucose with IV dextrose if symptomatic
Sick-day rules: double oral dose for fever, IM if vomiting
explanation.pearls: Hydrocortisone 200 mg over 24 hours provides sufficient mineralocorticoid activity that fludrocortisone is not needed acutely; restart fludrocortisone when oral intake resumes.

sources.label: Endocrine Society of Australia: Adrenal Insufficiency

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 38-year-old previously healthy man brought in with sudden onset 'thunderclap' headache, palpitations, drenching sweat and pallor. This is his third such paroxysm in 4 weeks. Today his BP swung from 220/130 to 90/52 within 30 minutes. CT brain excluded haemorrhage. His symptoms have settled but BP is currently 184/108. The endocrine team has been called and an adrenal incidentaloma 4.8 cm was noted on his CT 6 months ago.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 184/108 (currently), HR 116, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Pale, mildly diaphoretic, tremor, otherwise unremarkable; no goitre, no exophthalmos
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia, no ischaemic change; troponin not elevated; Na 138, K 4.0, glucose 7.4; 24-hour plasma metanephrines previously not done; CT brain normal; CT adrenal 4.8 cm right adrenal mass with washout suggesting phaeochromocytoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Commence labetalol 200 mg twice daily oral; admit and arrange laparoscopic adrenalectomy within 48 hours of admission
options.rationale: Labetalol has predominantly beta-blocking activity at typical oral doses and is not adequate alpha blockade; surgery within 48 hours without preparation has high crisis mortality. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence metoprolol 50 mg twice daily oral; titrate based on resting heart rate and clinic blood pressure measurements
options.rationale: Beta blockade without prior alpha blockade in phaeochromocytoma causes unopposed alpha vasoconstriction and life-threatening hypertensive crisis; this is the classic anti-pattern. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence amlodipine 5 mg daily and atenolol 50 mg daily; arrange outpatient endocrine review in 2 weeks for biochemistry
options.rationale: Calcium channel blocker plus beta blocker still includes beta blockade before alpha blockade and outpatient delay risks further crisis; admission is required for alpha blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily oral, titrate up over 10 to 14 days; defer beta blockade until alpha blockade established
options.rationale: Alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine is mandatory before any beta blockade in suspected phaeochromocytoma to prevent unopposed alpha stimulation and hypertensive emergency; surgery is planned 10 to 14 days after adequate alpha blockade. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Phaeochromocytoma)

options.text: Commence captopril 25 mg three times daily oral; arrange 24-hour fractionated metanephrines as outpatient before biopsy
options.rationale: ACE inhibitor does not address catecholamine excess; biopsy of suspected phaeochromocytoma can precipitate fatal crisis and is contraindicated. (Source: Endocrine Society of Australia: Phaeochromocytoma)

explanation.summary: Phaeochromocytoma should be confirmed biochemically with plasma free metanephrines or 24-hour urinary fractionated metanephrines and imaged with CT or MRI. Once suspected, preoperative alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine 10 mg twice daily titrated over 10 to 14 days is essential before considering beta blockade, which is reserved for persistent tachycardia after adequate alpha blockade. Surgery is planned only after adequate alpha blockade and volume repletion. Biopsy is contraindicated as it can precipitate catecholamine release. Genetic testing is offered to all phaeochromocytoma patients given the high prevalence of inherited syndromes such as MEN2, VHL, NF1 and SDH mutations.
key_points: Alpha blockade with phenoxybenzamine before any beta blockade
Surgery 10 to 14 days after adequate alpha blockade and volume repletion
Biopsy contraindicated in suspected phaeochromocytoma
Genetic testing for all phaeochromocytoma patients
explanation.pearls: Adequate alpha blockade is judged by mild orthostatic hypotension and nasal stuffiness; phenoxybenzamine is irreversible and accumulates, so taper carefully postoperatively to avoid hypotension.

stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 68-year-old postmenopausal woman in osteoporosis clinic. She had a fragility fracture of her right wrist after a fall from standing height 6 weeks ago. Her DEXA shows L1-L4 T-score minus 2.8 and femoral neck T-score minus 2.6. She has stable hypertension on perindopril and no GI symptoms. Her eGFR is 64 mL/min/1.73m^2. She is not lactose intolerant and lives independently.

data_table.Examination: Well; no spinal tenderness; right wrist healing; no peripheral oedema; gait stable with stick
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 2.42, phosphate 1.0, vitamin D 64 nmol/L, Cr 88, eGFR 64, TSH 1.8, PTH 4.4 (normal), DEXA T-scores as above, beta-CTX baseline 0.62
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg daily; vitamin D 1000 IU daily; calcium 600 mg twice daily; nil PPI

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological therapy?

options.text: Strontium ranelate 2 g orally daily; review cardiovascular risk and DEXA at 12 months; continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation
options.rationale: Strontium ranelate has been withdrawn from many markets and is not first-line due to cardiovascular safety concerns; not recommended in current AU practice. (Source: AMH: Bone disorders)

options.text: Denosumab 60 mg subcutaneously every 6 months; review at 12 months with DEXA and serial beta-CTX measurements
options.rationale: Denosumab is second line after bisphosphonate failure or intolerance, primarily because of the rebound vertebral fracture risk on discontinuation, requiring careful long-term planning. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

options.text: Teriparatide 20 micrograms subcutaneously daily for 18 months; sequence to oral bisphosphonate after the course completes
options.rationale: Teriparatide is reserved for severe osteoporosis with very low T-score, multiple vertebral fractures or bisphosphonate failure; not first-line. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

options.text: Raloxifene 60 mg orally daily; advise on VTE risk; review at 12 months with DEXA and bone turnover markers
options.rationale: Raloxifene reduces vertebral but not hip fractures and is reserved for women without vasomotor symptoms or VTE risk; not first-line after hip-relevant DEXA decline. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

options.text: Alendronate 70 mg orally weekly; review at 12 months with DEXA and beta-CTX; continue calcium and vitamin D supplementation
options.rationale: Oral bisphosphonate is first-line therapy for postmenopausal osteoporosis after fragility fracture with eGFR above 35; alendronate is PBS-funded and effective. (Source: RACGP Osteoporosis Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal osteoporosis with fragility fracture qualifies for pharmacotherapy regardless of T-score. Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate or risedronate) is first-line in the absence of significant GI disease or eGFR below 35. Denosumab is second-line and requires careful long-term planning due to rebound vertebral fracture on discontinuation. Adequate vitamin D and calcium intake are prerequisites and ongoing during therapy. Bone turnover markers and DEXA support monitoring of response.
key_points: Oral bisphosphonate is first-line for postmenopausal osteoporosis after fragility fracture
Denosumab requires careful discontinuation planning due to vertebral fracture rebound
Teriparatide is reserved for severe disease
Maintain calcium 1000 to 1300 mg/day and vitamin D 25(OH)D above 50 nmol/L
explanation.pearls: Atypical femur fractures and osteonecrosis of jaw are rare bisphosphonate adverse effects; consider drug holiday after 5 years of oral bisphosphonate in low-risk patients.
